questions.question: How many elements are in the single-qubit Pauli group G₁?
questions.explanation: G₁ = {±1, ±i} × {I, X, Y, Z} has 4 phases × 4 operators = 16 elements. These are: ±I, ±X, ±Y, ±Z, ±iI, ±iX, ±iY, ±iZ.

questions.question: What is the product XY?
questions.explanation: XY = iZ. This follows from the anticommutation relation: XY = -YX, and the fact that the Paulis form a group with specific multiplication rules. You can verify: [[0,1],[1,0]] × [[0,-i],[i,0]] = [[i,0],[0,-i]] = i×Z.

questions.question: Two Pauli operators P and Q anticommute if:
questions.explanation: Operators anticommute when PQ = -QP, or equivalently {P,Q} = PQ + QP = 0. For single-qubit Paulis, any two different non-identity Paulis anticommute.

questions.question: In the symplectic representation, how is the Pauli Y encoded?
questions.explanation: Y = iXZ, so it has both X and Z components. In the symplectic representation: I→(0,0), X→(1,0), Y→(1,1), Z→(0,1). The phase i is tracked separately.

questions.question: When do two n-qubit Paulis commute?

options.text: When they share at least one position
When the symplectic inner product is 0

questions.explanation: Two n-qubit Paulis commute iff their symplectic inner product ⟨v₁,v₂⟩_s = x₁·z₂ + z₁·x₂ = 0 (mod 2). This counts the number of positions where they have different non-identity operators; even count means commutation.

questions.question: How many distinct n-qubit Pauli operators are there (ignoring phase)?
questions.explanation: Each qubit can have I, X, Y, or Z (4 choices). For n qubits, there are 4ⁿ distinct Paulis ignoring phase. Including phases {±1, ±i}, the full group has 4×4ⁿ = 4ⁿ⁺¹ elements.

questions.question: The identity operator I commutes with all elements of the Pauli group.
questions.explanation: True. The identity I commutes with everything: IP = PI for any operator P. In fact, {±I, ±iI} forms the center of the Pauli group - the set of elements that commute with all others.
